executive_summary: The AI medical diagnostics space is a heavily validated but dominated market (59/100) with 17+ competitors including public companies Tempus ($12.8B market cap) and HeartFlow ($1.5B valuation), plus heavily funded players like Aidoc ($420M+), Viz.ai ($252M), and Lunit ($561M) — ALL operating with physician oversight; the only FDA-approved autonomous AI diagnostic (IDx-DR) covers diabetic retinopathy only, and multiple companies that aggressively pursued autonomous AI diagnosis (Babylon Health $4B, Olive AI $4B) have collapsed.
score_reasoning:
Score: 59/100
- Market Validation Signal: 18/20 (17+ competitors found: 9 direct, 5 indirect, 3 adjacent — clear mix of direct AI diagnostic companies, imaging incumbents, and adjacent symptom checkers validates an active, well-funded market)
- Competitive Opportunity: 2/20 (strongest competitor: Tempus AI — public company NASDAQ:TEM, $12.8B market cap, $1.27B revenue, 2000+ employees; runner-up: Aidoc — $420M+ funding, 1600+ hospitals, 20+ FDA clearances — market heavily dominated by well-capitalized incumbents)
- Market Gap Evidence: 12/20 (5 gaps identified from reviews: AI accuracy only 50-62% for symptom checkers, no autonomous approval for serious conditions, poor EHR integration, geographic access limits, cost barriers — at least 2 thematically related around accuracy/autonomy, but the key gap of autonomous diagnosis exists due to regulatory prohibition rather than market failure)
- Pricing Intelligence: 12/20 (pricing found for 4 competitors with meaningful detail: Ada Health free/$9.99/mo, K Health $29-$49/mo, HeartFlow per-procedure/public, enterprise models for Aidoc/Tempus/PathAI; consumer tier $0-$49/mo, enterprise tier $100K+/yr)
- Failure Intelligence: 15/20 (4 dead competitors with 3+ detailed post-mortems: Babylon Health 2023 $4B collapse, Olive AI 2023 $4B shutdown, Kintsugi 2026 $30M shutdown, Cydoc 2025 7-year failure; clear failure patterns: overpromised AI autonomy, unsustainable unit economics, regulatory runway mismatch, EHR integration costs)
Total: 18 + 2 + 12 + 12 + 15 = 59

direct_competitors.name: Tempus AI

pricing.detail: Public company (NASDAQ: TEM), $1.27B revenue in 2025, per-test pricing for genomic profiling, data licensing to pharma

strengths: Largest clinical+molecular data library in oncology
Public company with 83% YoY revenue growth
Acquired Paige AI ($81M) and Arterys for pathology+radiology
Multi-modal: genomics, digital pathology, clinical data
weaknesses: Focused on oncology, limited in cardiac/general diagnostics
direct_competitors.estimated_size: Public company, $12.8B market cap, $1.27B annual revenue

direct_competitors.name: Aidoc

pricing.range: Enterprise per-study or annual platform fee
pricing.detail: Custom enterprise pricing, available via AWS Marketplace, annual aiOS platform license

strengths: aiOS platform consolidates multiple AI algorithms
30% reduction in turnaround time for critical cases

direct_competitors.name: Viz.ai

pricing.detail: Platform licensing for care coordination, custom pricing per health system

strengths: AI-powered care coordination for time-sensitive conditions
Stroke detection with rapid alert to care teams
Strong integration with existing clinical workflows

direct_competitors.name: PathAI

pricing.range: Enterprise platform licensing + per-case for biopharma
pricing.detail: AISight platform for labs, partnership pricing for pharma clinical trials

strengths: Open ecosystem via AISight Link API

direct_competitors.name: Ada Health

pricing.detail: Free symptom assessment, Premium plan $9.99/mo with personalized health insights. Enterprise B2B for health systems.

strengths: AI symptom checker with personalized results
weaknesses: Not a diagnostic tool — triage/guidance only

traction.reviews: 4.5+ stars on app stores, Trustpilot reviews

direct_competitors.name: K Health

pricing.detail: $29/mo primary care access, $49/mo with mental health, $73/mo for weight loss program. No insurance required.

strengths: AI symptom checker is free entry point
weaknesses: AI assists but doesn't diagnose autonomously
Mental health limited to medication management

direct_competitors.name: HeartFlow

pricing.detail: Public company (NASDAQ: HTFL), per-procedure billing, UnitedHealthcare coverage secured. 70% gross margins.

strengths: Public company with 38% YoY revenue growth
AI converts CT images to 3D heart models
weaknesses: Requires coronary CTA imaging as input

direct_competitors.name: Digital Diagnostics (LumineticsCore/IDx-DR)

pricing.detail: FDA De Novo cleared autonomous AI. Reimbursed by CMS. Per-screening fee at point of care.

strengths: ONLY FDA-approved fully autonomous AI diagnostic
weaknesses: Cannot diagnose cancer, cardiac, or other conditions
Took 20+ years of research to get one approval
Scaling beyond one condition is extremely slow

direct_competitors.name: Lunit

pricing.range: Per-study or embedded in hardware licensing
pricing.detail: Available standalone or embedded in GE Healthcare, Philips, Fujifilm imaging equipment. Per-study cloud pricing.

strengths: 13% increased cancer detection in dense breasts (Lancet)
97% accuracy in chest X-ray cancer detection
Embedded in major imaging hardware (GE, Philips, Fujifilm)
weaknesses: Limited to chest X-ray and mammography primarily

indirect_competitors.name: Teladoc Health
indirect_competitors.approach: Telehealth platform connecting patients with licensed physicians via video/phone consultations
indirect_competitors.relevance: Solves access-to-diagnosis problem via physician telehealth, not autonomous AI. Major incumbent with $2B+ revenue.

indirect_competitors.name: Siemens Healthineers
indirect_competitors.approach: Medical device incumbent embedding AI into imaging hardware (AI-Pathway Companion, AI-Rad Companion)
indirect_competitors.relevance: Massive installed base of imaging equipment with integrated AI. Could dominate AI diagnostics through hardware lock-in.

indirect_competitors.name: Philips Healthcare
indirect_competitors.approach: Medical imaging incumbent with AI-powered CT tools, SmartSpeed MRI, Advanced Visualization Workspace
indirect_competitors.relevance: Similar to Siemens — embedded AI in existing hardware. Vendor-neutral approach enables broad adoption.

indirect_competitors.name: GE Healthcare
indirect_competitors.approach: Medical imaging with embedded AI capabilities across modalities
indirect_competitors.relevance: One of the 'big three' imaging incumbents. AI embedded in hardware gives massive distribution advantage.

indirect_competitors.name: Google Health (Med-PaLM/MedLM)
indirect_competitors.approach: Large language models (Med-PaLM, MedLM) for medical question answering and clinical decision support
indirect_competitors.relevance: Could pivot from research to clinical deployment. Google's scale and AI capabilities represent existential threat to startups.

adjacent_competitors.name: Docus AI
adjacent_competitors.current_focus: AI-powered lab test interpretation and clinical report generation for diagnostic laboratories
adjacent_competitors.pivot_threat: Could expand from lab result interpretation to full diagnostic assessment
adjacent_competitors.evidence: Already building doctor-ready AI clinical reports from lab data, adding differential diagnosis suggestions

adjacent_competitors.name: Ubie Health
adjacent_competitors.current_focus: AI-powered symptom checker and digital triage for patients and hospitals, based in Japan
adjacent_competitors.pivot_threat: Expanding globally from Japan, could move from triage to more assertive diagnostic suggestions
adjacent_competitors.evidence: Hospital deployment for digital triage, partnerships with health systems, global expansion underway

adjacent_competitors.name: Buoy Health
adjacent_competitors.current_focus: AI-powered symptom triage and care navigation
adjacent_competitors.pivot_threat: Could deepen diagnostic capabilities beyond triage
adjacent_competitors.evidence: $86.7M raised, partnerships with health systems for care navigation

failed_competitors.name: Babylon Health
failed_competitors.reason_failed: Collapsed after $4B valuation. Overpromised AI capabilities (critics noted basic decision trees rather than true AI), unsustainable unit economics, failed SPAC deal, difficult US market entry, regulatory reality collision. Raised $850M+ but ran massive losses.

failed_competitors.name: Olive AI
failed_competitors.reason_failed: Shut down after $4B valuation and $832M raised. Healthcare AI automation built on brittle RPA scripts that failed to scale. Overpromised technical capabilities, challenging economics, management missteps.

failed_competitors.name: Kintsugi
failed_competitors.reason_failed: Shut down after $30M raised and 7 years. AI voice biomarker for mental health screening. Regulatory pathway (FDA) was scientifically valid but economically fatal — runway measured in months while regulatory timelines measured in years.

failed_competitors.name: Cydoc
failed_competitors.reason_failed: Bootstrapped health AI startup shut down after 7 years. Broken business model: hosting+AI costs ~$70/doctor, customers willing to pay <$100/mo, EHR integrations cost $4-6K each. Lost 3 of 4 customers due to workflow friction, staff turnover, and scope creep.

market_gaps.gap: AI diagnostic accuracy insufficient for serious conditions — symptom checkers achieve only 50-62% accuracy, inadequate for cancer/cardiac diagnosis without physician oversight
sources: Nature systematic review of symptom checkers

market_gaps.gap: No autonomous AI diagnosis approved for serious conditions (cancer, cardiac) — only IDx-DR approved for diabetic retinopathy. Regulatory barrier, not market gap.
Poor EHR and workflow integration creates adoption friction — even validated AI tools lose customers due to integration costs ($4-6K per integration) and workflow disruption
sources: bekey.io failed AI pilots in hospitals

market_gaps.gap: Geographic accessibility — AI diagnostic tools concentrated in US/EU, limited availability in developing countries where physician shortages are most acute
Cost barriers for individual patients — enterprise pricing models ($100K+ annual contracts) exclude direct patient access; consumer tools provide only triage, not diagnosis

evidence.quote: Aidoc has raised over 420 million dollars in total funding, including a massive 150 million dollar Series E round in July 2025. The aiOS platform currently supports clinical care for more than 45 million patients annually across over 150 healthcare systems globally. Aidoc holds over 20 FDA clearances, more than any other company in the radiology AI category.
In the year 2025, Tempus AI had annual revenue of $1.27B with 83.41% growth. Market cap $12.8B.

I realized our business model was broken. Doctors in the small practices we had been targeting wanted to pay fewer than $100 per month. Hosting and AI costs had gone up to about $70 per doctor. Each EHR integration costs $4,000-$6,000. It would take 11 years per practice just to break even.
